0.name: Sam Fondacaro
bio: Hi! My name is Sam Fondacaro, I'm a second year Computer Science and Computational Math student. I'm also in RIT's Birding Club, which originally drew me to this initiative. It's really sad to me that birds die in droves from such an easily preventable issue, but at the same time I find it heartening that there are growing initiatives in universities across the country to remedy this issue. I love that this course allows me to apply the technical skills to solve a genuinely important problem.

I had a dual purpose in creating this website; I want to showcase my peers' brilliant projects that are the culmination of an entire semester of work, but I also want to get information out about the cause.

Whenever I talk to friends, at least those who haven't already listened to me bloviate about bird-window collisions, they routinely guess that the number of birds who die from this problem every year number in the tens of thousands. When I tell them that this number is off by several orders of magnitude, they are shocked. This is not by any means their fault. It simply tells me that this is not an issue that the public is aware of.

My hope is that people will see this website, find the projects as interesting as I do, and come away knowledgable and concerned about the problem facing our avian friends, in Rochester and across the country.
0.projectBlurb: Hello! Welcome to my website :D I used React JS with React Bootstrap components to make a replacement website, and I'm hoping to continue until I graduate.
0.shortenedBio: My name is Sam Fondacaro, I'm a second year Computer Science and Computational Math student. I'm also a member of the RIT Birding Club! I created this website as a portfolio for the work of my classmates, for this semester and the future. The opportunity to use my technical skills to make positive change excites me deeply.

1.name: Hayden Berner
bio: Hi! My name is Hayden Berner, and I'm a Master's student in Environmental Science. I earned my bachelor's degree in Environmental Science with a minor in Communications.

I've always had a strong appreciation for nature and animals, which continues to drive both my academic and personal interests. In the past I have interned at Seneca Park Zoo. I love spending time outdoors, especially hiking, and I enjoy volunteering to plant native trees and support local organisms and ecosystems.

This project aims to explore how members of the RIT community perceive the nature around campus, with a focus on birds and bird window collision interactions. Through an online survey (link to survey can be here also), we ask participants about their awareness of birds on campus, their experiences with bird collisions, and we ask about their thoughts & feelings with respect to wildlife in urban community spaces.

By understanding how people view and emotionally respond to these interactions with birds and their collisions with building windows, this project aims to highlight the relationship between people, infrastructure, and wildlife at RIT.  Gathering the perspectives of RIT community members in this survey and future work, can help inform current and future efforts by prioritizing humans sharing campus with wildlife while preserving our community's connection to its ideals and beliefs.
1.projectBlurb: Hayden spent the semester developing and deploying a Qualitirics survey to better understand the emotional impact of bird strikes.
1.shortenedBio: Hi! My name is Hayden Berner, and I'm a Master's student in Environmental Science. I earned my bachelor's degree in Environmental Science with a minor in Communications. This project aims to explore how members of the RIT community perceive the nature around campus, with a focus on birds and bird window collisions.

2.name: Dr. Richard Fadok (Course Instructor)
bio: Richard Fadok is the instructor of Smash the Crash, a vertically integrated project (VIP) course at RIT.
He is an assistant professor of anthropology at RIT, where his research examines how the built environment shapes everyday relations of violence, care, and justice between human and nonhuman animals.
He is also the founder and director of Smash the Crash, a university-community initiative to study and prevent bird-window collisions in Rochester, New York.
He has a PhD in History, Anthropology, and Science, Technology, and Society from MIT and held postdoctoral fellowships at the University of Pennsylvania and the University of Rochester.
2.shortenedBio: Richard Fadok is the instructor of Smash the Crash, a vertically integrated project (VIP) course at RIT. He is an assistant professor of anthropology at RIT. He is also the founder and director of Smash the Crash, a university-community initiative to study and prevent bird-window collisions in Rochester, New York.

3.name: Christopher Nokes
bio: My name is Christopher Nokes, I'm a senior in the Computer Engineering and Computer Science undergraduate programs here at RIT. My experience is primarily in embedded systems and hardware design and verification. I've had internships at Picatinny Arsenal, Ciena, Fresenius Medical Care, and Clarity Innovations, and have been doing research in hardware security since my third year.

My project is motivated by the lack of universal and consistent tools for measuring bird-window collisions. Existing survey methods are limited by human factors such as time and availability, while automated methods tend to either over-sample or under-sample collisions due to factors such as noise and sensor sensitivity. Accurate collision numbers create more convincing arguments for bird-safe regulations, and help ensure the efficacy of collision mitigation tools.
3.shortenedBio: Christopher Nokes is a senior in the Computer Engineering and Computer Science undergraduate programs, working on the automated detection of bird-window collisions using low-cost IoT devices.
3.projectBlurb: Chris spent the semester on an independent study, focused on the original idea of using his programming and computer engineering expertise to create an automated collision detector.

4.name: Ian Torres
bio: Hi! My name is Ian Torres, and I am a student at the Rochester Institute of Technology studying Humanities, Computation, and Design, with a background in cybersecurity and data analysis.

My work on Smash the Crash focuses on collecting, organizing, and visualizing bird collision data to better understand how these events occur across Rochester. I have been developing tools to process observation data, map collision hotspots, and create interactive visualizations that make these patterns easier to interpret and communicate.

This project is motivated by the challenge of making collision data both reliable and accessible. While there are many individual reports of bird-window collisions, they are often scattered, inconsistently recorded, or difficult to analyze at scale. By building a structured pipeline and visual mapping tools, my goal is to highlight where collisions are most concentrated and support more targeted, data-driven approaches to bird-safe design.
4.shortenedBio: Ian spent the semester developing data and mapping tools to better understand where bird collisions occur across Rochester. His work focuses on identifying hotspots and creating visualizations that support bird-safe design and awareness.
4.projectBlurb: Ian developed methods of displaying quantitative data about window strikes, culminating in a Story Map which is a centerpiece of the website that convincingly tells the story of bird-window collisions in Rochester.
